# Universal Themes: Common Concepts in Literature and Life

![universal theme of overcoming impossible odds](https://assets.ltkcontent.com/images/87232/universal-theme_27c5571306.jpg)

Theme refers to the central meaning or message in a work of literature. Sometimes it is overtly stated within the book, but often it is conveyed more indirectly with the author expecting readers to take away lessons or insights related to the theme from reading the work. Many works have universal themes, which are ones that are relevant to everyone. There are many universal themes in literature as well as life. After all, art imitates life — or is it the other way around?

## What Is a Universal Theme?

A universal theme is one that focuses on an element of the human condition and deals with concepts or concerns of interest to members of the human race, regardless of where they live, their background, personality type, or other unique factors.

In other words, universal themes appeal to or have meaning to people regardless of individual or cultural differences. Universal themes focus on common aspects of existence that, in some way, impact all people. The fact that they can be relevant to everyone is what makes them universal ideas.

## List of Universal Themes About Change

Change is a common universal [theme in literature](https://www.yourdictionary.com/articles/theme-examples-literature) and life.

## List of Universal Themes About Power

Universal themes related to power are common in literature and real life.

## List of Universal Themes About Romantic Love

The universal theme of love is at the heart of human relationships as well as many literary works.

## List of Universal Themes About Friendship and Family

Friendship and family are central to the human experience, so it's a common universal theme in literature and life.

## List of Universal Themes About Money

Money is a common universal theme in life and literature.

## List of Universal Themes About Knowledge and Education

Knowledge is a common universal theme in life and literature.

## List of Universal Themes About Politics/Freedom

There are many universal themes related to politics and society.

## List of Universal Themes About Technology

Technology is one of the most common universal themes. It's featured in science fiction and other [literary genres](https://www.yourdictionary.com/articles/examples-genres), as well as virtually every aspect of daily life.

## Theme and Motif in Literature

Even though this is a long list of universal themes and ideas, it's not all-inclusive. Many universal concepts pervade the human experience and find their way into literature. Of course, when you're focused on literature, theme isn't all that matters. It's also important to [consider motif](https://www.yourdictionary.com/articles/examples-motif-themes), which is often confused with theme. Explore [theme vs. motif](https://www.yourdictionary.com/articles/theme-motif-differences-connections) to help make sure you understand the difference.

FULL CONTENT:
# [Bookish Brews](https://bookishbrews.com)

Calling for a fundamental cultural shift through stories (with a side of your favorite brew)

# Literary Themes & the Myth of Universal Themes in Literature

![](https://i0.wp.com/bookishbrews.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Literary-Themes-Universality-Literature.jpg?fit=768%2C433&ssl=1)

Welcome friends! Today I’m talking to you about literary themes but more importantly the myth of universal themes in literature. In school many of us are taught that there are certain themes that can be present in literature that can connect all peoples because they’re core emotions or experiences of being human. But ultimately, as I’ve grown and read so much more, I’ve realized that no theme is completely universal. Not only that, but I’ve realized the benefit of reading about themes and experiences that I don’t relate to or quite understand. It helps me learn about others and grow my empathy to be encompassing of more experiences.

Universal themes are essentially only universal to a certain demographic of people and imply that the experiences of others are not as valid as those that are supposedly “universal.” But what if the idea that our stories need to include an experience that everyone understands is actually counterintuitive to our movements? What if we benefit more, as a society, from leaning into our different experiences? What if we all looked at books as windows into not just a new and imagined world, but someone else’s real life world? If you’re new here, I’m always trying to tear down some literary hierarchies, so let’s get started!

#### Table of Contents

## What are themes in literature?

Literary themes are the main ideas or underlying concepts within a novel. They are generally vaguer and touch on concepts that many people can relate to in order to make the story more relatable to a wider audience. Writers often show the theme through the plot, dialogue, characters, and setting. But the theme is not any of those aspects alone.

The literary theme of a novel is an overarching message or topic that is expressed throughout the entire story. It is often what the author is “really trying to say.” Themes can be as simple or as complex as makes sense for the story. For example, the theme can be “be true to yourself”. However, it can also be used to explain or explore deeper fundamental issues of humanity and/or society. Or both!

Many people will call literary themes “universal” because of their ability to connect with a wider audience, but there are many themes that are not universal in nature. For example, not everyone will personally relate to a theme of immigration or war. Especially if they have not been close to either in their lifetimes. Themes can help transcend differences and build a connection across peoples. However, they can also be used to connect with people who are similar to you. Though themes are very general aspects of human life, we do not experience themes in the same way. Paying attention to literary themes is a beautiful way for us to understand how different people react to different life events.

![a photo of a very white library featured in a post about Literary Themes & the Myth of Universal Themes in Literature](https://i0.wp.com/bookishbrews.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/Book-Border-1.jpg?resize=840%2C153&ssl=1)

## How do we find themes in books?

FInding themes in novels can be difficult because they are subjective. Each reader comes in with their own prior life experiences and therefore may take out a different theme from a novel. This is one of the most beautiful things about reading! Each reader can read an entirely different story while reading the same words.

A story about a teenager whose uncle is in the hospital during a major breakthrough in her growth into adulthood is interpreted completely differently by different readers. Some readers may be more familiar with the coming of age theme. This is especially true if they haven’t experienced death in their family before. While other readers may have recently suffered a loss in their family and they may connect the most with the theme of grief.

## Common Literary Themes

There are a wide variety of common literary themes but I’ll list a few that are common in Western canon:

## Why Do We Use Themes in Literature?

Literary themes are general and meant to appeal to a wider audience. For example, not everyone will relate to a main character that is a famous actor whose co-star gets murdered. However, they might connect to an overarching literary theme such as death or grief. Themes are what helps us connect to stories that we may not otherwise connect with. This means that they help more readers connect with the same story in different ways.

Some themes are more general and can help reach an even wider audience. There are certain themes that are so general that we call them universal themes. They transcend time, culture, differences, location, etc and they are something that connects us all through the virtue of being human. These themes help us, as readers, to be able to revisit older works. They keep the work alive in perpetuity because they include aspects of human life that many people can connect with. Theoretically, this is why people are able to connect to some very old novels without them becoming vastly outdated. We call these universal themes.

### What are universal themes in literature?

Theoretically, universal themes are themes that are essential to the human condition. They are experiences that we all have and therefore they are able to bridge the divisions between cultures and geographic locations. As an example, universal themes can include topics such as grief, safety, and family. They’re generally very vague ideas that can connect over a wide variety of narratives and audiences. The idea is that all universal themes are central to human nature and therefore we all experience them.

In an ideal world, universal themes are themes that are written into books (and other media!) that will help make that book relatable to a wider audience, in turn, selling more copies. Everyone experiences grief at some point in their life, so including grief as a major theme in your novel may help more readers connect to the story.

![a banner of open books shot from above featured in a post about Literary Themes & the Myth of Universal Themes in Literature](https://i0.wp.com/bookishbrews.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/pages-border.jpg?resize=840%2C153&ssl=1)

## The Myth of Universality in Literature

One thing that I noticed when I was doing my research for this post on themes is the encouragement to make themes general. Lists that praise the “best themes” to write about or articles praising universal themes in literature. Theoretically, they transcend time, experience, location, and culture. But it makes me wonder whether universality in literature is even real. Universal themes are generalized so much that they start to lose all meaning all for the sake of being able to call them universal. But is universality even good? What does a story about “love” even mean?

## Is Universality in Literature Bad?

Historically and even today, there is one prominent voice in literature. People from one single background are primarily driving the canon of today. The way that people with similar backgrounds experience love may all be very similar, but is it similar to the way that people from different backgrounds experience “love”? If we are only exposed to experiencing “love” in a certain way, will we start to think that other ways to experience and express love are less valid? Will we start to believe that only one way to “love” is the right way?

What, for example, happens after years and years of only being exposed to familial love from people of similar backgrounds and cultures? What happens when we are finally able to be exposed to experiencing and expressing love in a different way? How often have I heard an Asian American novel about a family being criticized because their parents don’t express love in a way that is familiar to the reader? How many times have I seen asexual characters be criticized for their friendships not “having enough romance in them”?

## Being Cautious of Your Themes

To me, one of the most exceptional thing about art is how people can interpret it in completely different ways. Even differently than how the author intended. Sometimes there are themes within a novel that a reader may pick up on but the author did not intend to write at all. The same is true about any other kind of art. The way we experience art can be completely different from one person to the next.

Though I find this to be truly exceptional, I also think that it is something to be wary of. Artists will always bring their backgrounds and identities into their art and writing is no exception. Similarly, artists can only speak about their experiences and may be completely unaware of their own biases. Artists are human and we all have our own biases, whether we are conscious of them or not.

For example, a fantasy novel about princesses in a foreign land may initially sound harmless, but these stories are so often intertwined with themes of imperialism and orientalism. It’s important to recognize that stories about royalty are often based on our most famous royal family, who have direct influence on much of the world’s imperialism and colonialism.

It’s hard to make an argument for universal themes when one person may read this fantasy novel and think that it’s about love and wealth. While another person will read the novel and think about atrocities that may have been committed upon their ancestors. While the person who relates to the themes of love and wealth may not even notice the imperialist themes. Everyone is different and brings their own backgrounds and experiences into reading, and therefore everyone perceives themes differently or even sees different themes altogether.

![](https://i0.wp.com/bookishbrews.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/Book-Border.jpg?resize=840%2C153&ssl=1)

## Can Universal Themes Truly Be Achieved?

Honestly, I can’t tell you. What I do believe is that we don’t need to have universality in literature. Books do not need to be written for a universal audience. We do not need to diminish themes to the point of meaninglessness. Our themes can be specific themes that will touch the hearts of specific readers. Your story will not be any less impactful because of it.

What I also believe is that it’s crucial to hear stories from people of different backgrounds. It’s important for us to understand that, though a theme may be considered universal, it can be expressed and experienced in different ways. Love is expressed in infinite amounts of ways and none of them are less valid than the other.

All of this is to say that it is important for readers to read books by authors from diverse backgrounds. And it is just as important for all of you writers out there to write books that represent who you are. Write your truth.

#### Decolonize Your Bookshelf With Me

Hi! I’m Amanda. Bookish Brews started as a personal project to decolonize my bookshelf turned into a passion for diverse stories. Once I realized how much we can grow personally from stories by people with different experiences than our own, I realized how much they impact our world. But I also know that growth from stories does not happen without intentionality. Bookish Brews is dedicated to building meaningful conversations about how stories by diverse voices can change our lives, our culture, and our world.

"Let's change the system via the lens of compelling fiction."

# 25 Universal Themes That Create Unforgettable Literature

[![Picture of Writing Team](https://www.hireawriter.us/hubfs/HAW%20Logo%20T%20%26%20White%20square-3.png) Writing Team](https://www.hireawriter.us/storytelling/author/writing-team) :

Jun 16, 2025 9:00:00 AM

![Picture of Writing Team](https://www.hireawriter.us/hubfs/HAW%20Logo%20T%20%26%20White%20square-3.png)
![25 Universal Themes That Create Unforgettable Literature](https://www.hireawriter.us/hubfs/HAW%20blog%20%2811%29-Jun-09-2025-07-34-30-3557-PM.png)

Every writer faces the same daunting question: what makes readers care about made-up people doing fictional things? The answer lies not in clever plot twists or beautiful prose, but in something deeper—universal themes that connect imaginary events to real human experience. These recurring ideas transform entertainment into art, stories into mirrors that reflect our deepest truths.

Theme is literature's secret weapon, the invisible force that makes readers weep over characters they've never met and remember books decades after reading them. Understanding how master writers employ these themes can elevate your own work from forgettable fiction to lasting literature.

## Understanding Literary Theme

Theme represents the core message or underlying idea that gives your story meaning beyond its surface events. While plot asks "what happens next?", theme asks "why does this matter?" It's the difference between a sequence of events and a meaningful exploration of human experience.

Themes work on multiple levels simultaneously. [*To Kill a Mockingbird* tells the surface story of a lawyer](https://torchbearer.utk.edu/2015/07/literature-law-and-atticus-finch/) defending a Black man in 1930s Alabama, but its deeper themes explore justice, prejudice, and moral courage. Readers remember the thematic resonance long after forgetting specific plot details.

The most effective themes feel universal—they tap into experiences and emotions that transcend cultural boundaries. Love, death, family, identity, and justice matter to readers regardless of their background because these concepts shape every human life. Writers who understand this principle can create stories that speak to diverse audiences across time and geography.

## The Power Dynamics of Good Versus Evil

The eternal conflict between good and evil remains literature's most enduring theme because it reflects humanity's fundamental moral struggles. This theme works best when writers avoid simplistic black-and-white characterizations, instead exploring the complex motivations that drive both heroic and villainous behavior.

*Harry Potter* succeeds not just because Harry fights Voldemort, but because each character grapples with moral choices throughout the series. Even minor characters like Snape embody the theme's complexity—his allegiances shift based on love, guilt, and redemption rather than simple good-versus-evil categories.

Contemporary writers often subvert this theme by blurring traditional moral boundaries. In *Breaking Bad*, Walter White begins as a sympathetic teacher but gradually becomes the villain of his own story. This evolution demonstrates how good versus evil can drive character development rather than simply providing external conflict.

## Love's Many Faces

Love themes dominate literature because romantic relationships represent one of humanity's most intense emotional experiences. However, the most memorable love stories go beyond simple romance to explore love's complexity, power, and consequences.

**Forbidden love** creates natural conflict by placing desire against social expectations. *Romeo and Juliet* endures because Shakespeare explored how young passion confronts family loyalty and social order. The theme works because readers understand the pain of wanting something they cannot have.

**Fated love** suggests that some relationships transcend ordinary circumstances. *Outlander* uses time travel to literalize this concept—Jamie and Claire's connection survives centuries because their bond exists beyond normal limitations. This theme appeals to readers' desire for meaningful connections in an uncertain world.

**Sacrificial love** demonstrates love's transformative power through characters who give up something valuable for others' happiness. O. Henry's "The Gift of the Magi" remains popular because it shows how true love involves selflessness rather than mere desire.

## Coming of Age and Self-Discovery

Coming-of-age stories resonate because everyone experiences the transition from innocence to experience. This theme works whether characters are literally growing up or simply maturing through challenging circumstances.

*Jane Eyre* exemplifies this theme by showing how adversity shapes character. Jane doesn't just age physically throughout the novel—she develops self-worth, independence, and emotional strength. Her journey from powerless orphan to confident woman mirrors readers' own struggles for identity and autonomy.

Modern coming-of-age stories often focus on specific identity aspects—sexual orientation, cultural heritage, or personal values. These variations on the classic theme help contemporary readers see their own experiences reflected in literature.

## Justice and Its Complications

[Justice themes](/storytelling/literary-motifs-what-is-a-motif-in-literature) explore humanity's deep need for fairness while acknowledging how rarely true justice occurs. These stories work because they tap into readers' frustration with real-world inequities.

**Righteous justice** stories satisfy readers by showing deserving characters receive appropriate rewards or punishments. *To Kill a Mockingbird* gains power from Atticus Finch's moral stand against racism, even though the immediate outcome disappoints.

**Unrighteous justice** or revenge themes warn against the corrupting effects of vengeance. *Wuthering Heights* demonstrates how Heathcliff's quest for justice destroys everyone around him, including himself. These cautionary tales resonate because they reflect the temptation to respond to injury with injury.

## The American Dream and Its Shadows

This theme explores the gap between idealistic expectations and harsh realities, particularly relevant to American literature but applicable wherever people chase unrealistic dreams.

*The Great Gatsby* remains literature's definitive exploration of this theme because Fitzgerald showed how wealth and status fail to provide happiness or meaning. Jay Gatsby's magnificent parties cannot win Daisy's love or erase his humble origins. The novel's enduring popularity stems from its revelation that material success often masks spiritual emptiness.

Contemporary writers adapt this theme to explore modern dream-chasing—startup culture, social media fame, or professional achievement. The core message remains constant: external success cannot fix internal problems.

## Mortality and Life's Meaning

Death themes force characters and readers to confront life's ultimate questions. These stories work because mortality affects everyone, regardless of age, culture, or circumstances.

[**The circle of life** theme](https://www.goodreads.com/shelf/show/circle-of-life) presents death as natural and necessary. *Charlotte's Web* helps young readers understand mortality by showing how death enables new life—Charlotte's sacrifice saves Wilbur while her offspring continue her legacy.

**The inevitability of death** creates tension through characters who fight against mortality. *The Picture of Dorian Gray* explores the corruption that results from trying to escape natural limits. These stories suggest that accepting mortality leads to more meaningful living.

## Family Dynamics and Chosen Bonds

Family themes examine how relationships shape identity and behavior. Modern literature often expands family definitions to include chosen relationships rather than just blood connections.

**Family dynamics** stories explore how family members influence each other, often across generations. [*Anna Karenina* shows how different family approaches](https://www.reddit.com/r/tolstoy/comments/1cn62f8/anna_karenina_happy_families/)—love versus duty, tradition versus change—produce dramatically different outcomes.

**The power of family** demonstrates how strong relationships provide strength during difficulties. *Little Women* succeeds because the March sisters support each other through poverty, illness, and separation. Their bond enables individual growth while maintaining family unity.

## Pride, Faith, and Human Nature

**Hubris** themes warn against excessive pride by showing how overconfidence leads to destruction. *Frankenstein* remains relevant because Victor's scientific arrogance reflects contemporary concerns about technological advancement without ethical consideration.

**Faith** themes explore how belief systems—religious, political, or personal—guide behavior and provide meaning. *The Poisonwood Bible* examines what happens when unwavering faith confronts complex reality, forcing characters to question their fundamental assumptions.

[![STORYTELLING BLOG SUB](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/24409333/5d1dd80d-52ca-4cf6-b803-547b8afa2e04.png)](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/24409333/5d1dd80d-52ca-4cf6-b803-547b8afa2e04)

![STORYTELLING BLOG SUB](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/24409333/5d1dd80d-52ca-4cf6-b803-547b8afa2e04.png)

## Individual Versus Society

These themes explore tension between personal desires and social expectations, particularly relevant in an interconnected world where individual choices affect entire communities.

**Self-identity** stories follow characters discovering who they are independent of others' expectations. These narratives resonate because everyone struggles to balance authentic self-expression with social acceptance.

**The influence of the mob** demonstrates how group thinking can override individual judgment. *The Lottery* horrifies readers because it shows how ordinary people can participate in terrible actions when social pressure demands conformity.

## Survival and Heroism

**Survival** themes test characters' resilience against overwhelming odds. *The Hunger Games* works because Katniss's physical survival mirrors many readers' emotional survival of difficult circumstances. These stories inspire because they show human determination overcoming seemingly impossible challenges.

**Heroism** themes celebrate courage and sacrifice for worthy causes. *The Lord of the Rings* endures because multiple characters demonstrate different types of heroism—physical courage, moral steadfastness, and selfless friendship.

## Redemption and Second Chances

**Redemption** themes offer hope by showing how people can overcome past mistakes through growth and atonement. *Atonement* demonstrates how one childhood error can reverberate across decades, but also how understanding and forgiveness can provide healing.

These stories work because everyone has regrets and hopes for second chances. Literature provides vicarious experiences of redemption that readers find emotionally satisfying and personally encouraging.

## Choosing Your Story's Theme

The most effective themes emerge naturally from character struggles rather than being imposed from outside. Ask yourself what fundamental human experiences your characters face, then develop plot events that explore those experiences from multiple angles.

Remember that [themes work best](/storytelling/the-role-of-themes-in-fiction-adding-depth-and-meaning-to-your-stories) when they're felt rather than explicitly stated. Let your characters' choices and consequences reveal your theme instead of having characters announce it directly. Readers prefer discovering meaning through story rather than being lectured about it.

## Transform Your Writing with Universal Themes

Understanding these twenty-five themes provides a foundation for [creating stories](/creative/creating-linguistic-constraints-writing-entire-stories-with-only-one-vowel-or-using-palindromes) that resonate across diverse audiences. The most successful writers don't just tell what happened—they explore why it matters. By grounding your fictional events in universal human experiences, you transform entertainment into art that speaks to readers' deepest needs and desires.

Ready to infuse your writing with themes that create lasting emotional impact? Our expert content creators at Hire a Writer understand how to weave universal themes naturally into compelling narratives. From concept development to final revision, we help writers create stories that not only entertain but also provide the meaningful experiences readers crave and remember.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Universal themes transcend cultural boundaries, addressing fundamental human experiences like love, mortality, and identity, allowing readers to connect deeply with diverse narratives.
Identity themes focus on the exploration of self, including personal growth, cultural heritage, and societal roles, reflecting the complexities of individual experiences across different backgrounds.
Conflict serves as a central element in literature, embodying struggles between characters, societies, and internal dilemmas, highlighting the human condition and moral challenges faced.

enriched_points.point_title: Nature as a Backdrop and Character
enriched_points.expanded_content: Nature often serves as both a setting and a character, influencing plot developments and embodying themes of beauty, danger, and the interconnectedness of life.
Cultural contexts shape the interpretation of literary themes, offering diverse perspectives on love, justice, and morality, enriching the understanding of universal concepts.
Literary themes evolve alongside societal changes, reflecting shifts in values, beliefs, and human experiences, allowing literature to remain relevant across generations.
